blurb: Colorado's own state cactus — a mounding hedgehog cactus that erupts in brilliant scarlet cups mid-spring through early summer, drawing hummingbirds to its nectar-rich flowers while thriving in the leanest, rockiest, driest ground the Front Range offers.
water: Very low; xeric once established — no supplemental water needed in most Front Range settings
spread: Slowly widens by offsets into a dense multi-stemmed mound; non-invasive
seasons: Evergreen ribbed cylindrical stems year-round; scarlet funnel flowers Apr–Jun; small reddish fruit Jul–Aug; ribbed green mound persists through winter (stems shrivel in cold, plump back up in spring)
wildlife: Premier early hummingbird plant — broad-tailed and rufous hummingbirds are the primary pollinators; bees also work the flowers

care.hardiness: Exceptionally cold-hardy for a cactus — the Front Range form of *Echinocereus triglochidiatus* tolerates USDA zone 5 and lower, withstanding temperatures to at least −10°F and arguably to −20°F for short periods in proven Colorado populations. It is Colorado's official state cactus, native to the foothills and mesas at 4,000–8,000 ft elevation, and is perfectly adapted to the Front Range climate (zones 5b–6a). The critical caveat: it is cold-hardy only when dormant and dry. Wet soil in winter dramatically increases winter-kill risk — cold plus wet = rot. A dry, cold winter poses no threat to a well-sited plant.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring (late Apr–May) once soil has warmed, or very early fall (Aug–early Sep) to allow root establishment before cold. Choose the sunniest, sharpest-draining spot available — a south- or west-facing rocky slope, a raised bed, or a crevice between boulders is ideal. Set the crown level with the surrounding grade or slightly above it; never bury the base. A thick gravel mulch (pea gravel or crushed granite) around the stems keeps the crown dry and mimics its natural scree habitat. In containers, use a deep clay pot with multiple drainage holes and a gritty cactus mix (50% perlite or crushed granite); position pots under a roof overhang or house eave in winter so rain and snowmelt can't saturate the mix — a wet frozen pot will kill it.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun is non-negotiable — 6 or more hours of direct sun daily. Colorado's intense high-altitude UV and low humidity are exactly what this plant evolved for. In shade it grows slowly, flowers poorly, and becomes vulnerable to rot. South- and southwest-facing exposures are best.[1][2]
care.soil: Well-drained, lean, gravelly or rocky soil is the single most important requirement. Sandy or gritty soil with a near-neutral to slightly alkaline pH suits it perfectly. It naturally grows in rocky outcrops, sandstone talus, and shallow soils over bedrock. In heavier Front Range clay, create a raised area or raised bed and amend deeply with coarse grit (30–50% by volume) to break up the clay structure; add no compost or organic matter, which retains moisture and promotes rot. The plant is adapted to alkaline soils — our clay-loam is no problem once drainage is assured.[1][2]
care.water: Once established (1–2 seasons), supplemental water is rarely needed on the Front Range — natural precipitation is sufficient. New transplants appreciate deep watering every 2–3 weeks during the first summer to help roots establish. During establishment, water from spring to early fall, then stop entirely in late September. Established plants should receive no supplemental irrigation from October through April, and minimal water (once a month at most, if soils are completely dry) during summer heat waves. The stems visibly shrink and wrinkle in winter dormancy — this is normal and the plant plumps back up in spring; do not water in response to shriveling in cold months.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space transplants 18–24 in apart. Individual clumps grow slowly — a few inches per year — eventually reaching 1–3 ft across over many years. Leave room for the mound to expand, but avoid crowding with aggressive neighbor plants.[1][2]
care.propagation: **By offsets (easiest):** In late spring or early summer, detach a mature offset (arm) from the parent clump using clean gloved hands or tongs — cut or twist cleanly at the base. Set aside in a warm, dry, shaded spot for 5–7 days for the cut end to callous. Plant into barely moist gritty cactus mix, propped upright, and water sparingly until roots form (4–6 weeks). **By seed:** Sow in late winter or early spring in a very gritty mix; surface-sow the seeds (light aids germination) and keep barely moist at 65–75°F. Germination is slow and irregular (4–8 weeks); seedlings grow very slowly and typically take 5+ years to bloom. A 4–6 week cold-moist stratification at 35–40°F before sowing can improve germination rates.[1][2]
care.bloom: Mid-April through early June on the Front Range (timing varies by elevation — lower sites bloom earlier). The large (up to 3 in wide) waxy scarlet-to-crimson funnel flowers emerge from the sides of the stems, multiple per stem, often covering the whole mound at peak. Individual blooms last 2–3 days and stay open through nights, making this one of the premier early hummingbird plants in Colorado. No deadheading needed — spent flowers drop cleanly and are followed by small reddish fruit.[1][2]
care.feeding: No fertilizing is needed or recommended for an established in-ground plant — lean soil is preferred and fertilizing encourages lush growth that is more rot-prone. For container-grown plants, a single light application of a low-nitrogen cactus fertilizer (high potassium, e.g. 0-10-10 or 2-7-7) in spring is acceptable; do not feed after midsummer.[2]
care.maintenance: Virtually zero once established. Remove any winter-killed stems in mid-spring with clean gloved tongs — cut back to live green tissue. Handle with thick leather gloves and long tongs; the stiff central spines are fixed but the smaller radial spines can be sharp. A gravel collar applied annually keeps the crown dry. There is no need to prune, divide, or deadhead.[1][2]
care.troubles: The primary cause of failure is crown and root rot from winter wetness — any site where rain or snowmelt pools around the crown will eventually kill the plant. Ensure perfect drainage from day one; raise the planting site if needed. Scale insects occasionally colonize the stems (white cottony patches at the areoles) — treat with isopropyl alcohol on a swab or neem oil, applied carefully to avoid damaging the spines. Mealybugs are rare but can occur. The plant is naturally pest- and disease-resistant in dry, sunny conditions. Do not use overhead irrigation — wet stems harbor fungal rot.[1][2]

edible.card: Ripe fruit (despine first — burn or rub off spines before eating)
edible.summary: The ripe berry-like fruit is edible and reportedly sweet-tasting; Native peoples of the Southwest ate it fresh and prepared it in various ways.[1][3]
edible.parts: The fruit (a fleshy berry, 15–35 mm, ripening reddish) is the edible part, available roughly 2 months after the spring bloom (Jul–Aug). At full ripeness the spines loosen and can be rubbed or burned off. The flesh is white to pale, sweet, and juicy — regarded as among the most flavorful of all Southwestern cactus fruits. Stems have been used ethnobotanically but require elaborate processing and are not a practical food for gardeners.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Wear thick gloves and use tongs to harvest ripe fruit. Remove spines completely before eating — the traditional method is to burn them off over a flame or rub them off on a rough surface; handle with care as residual spines and glochid-like clusters at the fruit tip can embed in lips and throat. Eat fresh out of hand once despined, or cook down into jam or syrup. The flesh is sweet enough to eat raw when fully ripe. Do not eat unripe fruit (spines firmly embedded, flesh unpalatable).[1][2][3]
edible.caution: The spines and any small spine clusters (at the fruit's areoles) are the primary hazard — they are stiff and can embed in skin, lips, and throat if the fruit is eaten without thorough spine removal first. This is different from Opuntia's detachable glochids but still requires careful preparation. No known toxicity in the fruit flesh itself; this is a well-documented traditional food of multiple Southwestern Indigenous nations. Children should not handle or eat the fruit without adult supervision due to spine risk. The stems and roots are not edible and contain compounds that make them unpalatable raw.[1][2][3]
